title: Swish in the Dark: Blindfolded Basketball Feat
story: Swoosh after swoosh echoed through the gym as Fred Newman achieved an incredible basketball feat. On February 5, 1978, he sank 88 free throws in a row while completely blindfolded! Newman's amazing accuracy and concentration allowed him to keep scoring even without seeing the hoop. This jaw-dropping display of skill showed that practice and focus can lead to extraordinary results, even when facing unusual challenges.

title: Jiggly Giant: World's Biggest Jell-O
story: Imagine a pool filled with wiggly, wobbly Jell-O! On February 5, 1981, Brisbane, Australia, became the home of the world's largest Jell-O creation. Chefs mixed up a massive batch of watermelon-flavored gelatin, using enough to fill several swimming pools. This gigantic jiggly treat contained 9,246 gallons of gelatin. That's more Jell-O than most people see in their entire lives!
activity_question: What flavor was the record-breaking Jell-O?
follow_up_question: How do you think they kept such a huge amount of Jell-O from melting?

title: Moonwalkers Make History
story: Astronauts Alan Shepard and Edward Mitchell took giant leaps for mankind on February 5, 1971. As part of the Apollo 14 mission, they became the third team of Americans to walk on the Moon. Their lunar stroll near Fra Mauro lasted about four hours. During this time, they collected Moon rocks, set up experiments, and even hit a few golf balls! Their successful mission helped pave the way for future space exploration.
activity_question: How long did the astronauts walk on the Moon?
bonus_fact: Alan Shepard was the first American in space, ten years before his Moon walk.

story: Hold on tight! In 1901, thrill-seekers got a wild new ride. Edwin Prescott built a roller coaster that did something amazing - it turned riders upside down! title: Newton's Giant Leap in Science
story: In 1676, Isaac Newton penned a famous letter that changed science forever. The brilliant thinker wrote to his fellow scientist Robert Hooke, sharing a powerful idea. Newton humbly said, 'If I have seen further, it is by standing on the shoulders of Giants.' He meant that his discoveries built on the work of great minds before him. This quote, written on February 5, still inspires scientists today.
activity_question: What did Newton mean by his famous quote?

He could see farther than most people
He liked to climb on giant statues
He was taller than other scientists
activity_answer: His discoveries built on the work of others

title: Golden Giant Unearthed in Australia
story: Imagine striking gold - literally! In 1869, two lucky miners stumbled upon a massive treasure. John Deason and Richard Oates were digging in Moliagul, Australia on February 5 when they uncovered the Welcome Stranger. This enormous chunk of gold weighed a whopping 97.14 kg, making it the largest alluvial gold nugget ever found. The discovery sent shockwaves through the mining world and put Australia on the map as a golden wonderland.
activity_question: What was the name of the giant gold nugget found in Australia?
follow_up_question: Can you guess how many regular gold rings could be made from a 97.14 kg gold nugget?

title: Charlie Chaplin's Hilarious Silent Masterpiece
story: Laughter filled theaters as Charlie Chaplin's silent film 'Modern Times' hit the big screen on February 5, 1936. This comedy genius wrote, directed, and starred in the movie. Chaplin's famous character, the Little Tramp, struggled hilariously with the challenges of modern life and factory work. The film cleverly poked fun at the fast-paced world of machines and assembly lines.
activity_question: What was Charlie Chaplin's role in the film 'Modern Times'?
activity_choices: He wrote, directed, and starred in it
He was just the main actor
He wrote the music for it
activity_answer: He wrote, directed, and starred in it
bonus_fact: Though a silent film, 'Modern Times' included sound effects and Chaplin's first on-screen speaking.

title: Silent Stars Unite for Movie Magic
story: Lights, camera, action! In 1919, four of the biggest names in silent films joined forces. Charlie Chaplin, Douglas Fairbanks, Mary Pickford, and D. W. Griffith founded United Artists on February 5. These Hollywood legends wanted more control over their movies. Their new studio would change the film industry forever.
activity_question: Who were the four founders of United Artists?
activity_choices: Charlie Chaplin, Douglas Fairbanks, Mary Pickford, and D. W. Griffith
Buster Keaton, Harold Lloyd, Lillian Gish, and Cecil B. DeMille
Rudolph Valentino, Gloria Swanson, Clara Bow, and Lon Chaney
Tom Mix, Theda Bara, Wallace Reid, and Fatty Arbuckle
activity_answer: Charlie Chaplin, Douglas Fairbanks, Mary Pickford, and D. W. Griffith
bonus_fact: United Artists' logo features the letters UA inside a rectangle, symbolizing a movie screen.

title: Slam Dunk for Equality
story: Swish! Bob Douglas scored a historic victory for basketball and civil rights. In 1972, he became the first African American elected to the Basketball Hall of Fame. His February 5 induction celebrated his skills and his role in breaking down racial barriers in sports.

story: Shoppers in Britain got a taste of the future in 1996. On February 5, supermarkets began selling a new kind of tomato puree. This wasn't just any sauce - it was made from genetically modified tomatoes! Scientists had tweaked the tomatoes' genes to make them extra tasty. It was the first time people in the UK could buy GM food at the store.
activity_question: What type of food product was the first genetically modified item sold in UK supermarkets?
follow_up_question: Why do you think scientists might want to modify the genes of food?

title: Tractor Tech Takes a Giant Leap
story: Farmers rejoiced on February 5, 1936, when Harry Ferguson revolutionized agriculture. He patented a clever hydraulic system for tractors that made controlling attachments a breeze. This invention allowed farmers to easily manage plows, seeders, and other tools hooked up to their tractors. Ferguson's smart design boosted farming efficiency and changed the face of agriculture worldwide.
activity_question: What did Harry Ferguson patent in 1936?
activity_choices: A suction side hydraulic control system
A new type of tractor engine
activity_answer: A suction side hydraulic control system
bonus_fact: This system is still known as the 'three-point hitch' and is used on most modern tractors today.

title: Timekeeping Tunes: The BBC Pips Begin
story: Beep, beep, beep! In 1924, a new sound hit the airwaves. The Royal Greenwich Observatory started broadcasting short, precise tones called the Greenwich Time Signal. These became known as the 'BBC pips'. On February 5, listeners first heard these hourly beeps, helping everyone keep accurate time. The pips quickly became a beloved part of British radio.
activity_question: What were the short, precise tones called?
follow_up_question: Why do you think accurate time signals were important for radio listeners?

title: Wildlife Warriors Unite!
story: Critters and creatures across America gained powerful allies in 1936. On February 5 that year, a group of nature lovers banded together to form the National Wildlife Federation. Their mission? title: Joni Mitchell's Musical Legacy Honored
story: Joni Mitchell's soulful voice and poetic lyrics earned her a special place in music history. On February 5, 1981, she was welcomed into the Canadian Music Hall of Fame. This honor celebrated her incredible talent as a singer and songwriter. Mitchell's songs, like 'Big Yellow Taxi,' touched hearts and inspired fans around the world.
activity_question: What honor did Joni Mitchell receive in 1981?
activity_choices: Inducted into the Canadian Music Hall of Fame
activity_answer: Inducted into the Canadian Music Hall of Fame
bonus_fact: Joni Mitchell is also a talented painter who has created many of her own album covers.
